coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional nanoparticles based on marine polysaccharides for apremilast delivery to inflammatory macrophages: Preparation, targeting ability, and uptake mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Shabana, Samah
Hamouda, Hamed I.
Abdalla, Mohnad
Sharaf, Mohamed
Chi, Zhe
Liu, Chenguang
coredata.dc:description: Hydrophobic drug encapsulation inside targeted nanoparticles can enhance accumulation in inflamed sites, limit toxicity to healthy tissue, and improve pharmacokinetics compared to free drug dosing. This study reports a functionalized marine polysaccharide nanoparticle with a controlled release, targeting abilities, and in-situ imaging properties. Carbon dots functionalized Enteromorpha polysaccharide/Mannose/Methionine functionalized Chitosan (CDs.EP/Man/Meth.Cs) NPs could deliver apremilast to inflammatory macrophages and Caco-2 intestinal cells as an in vitro model for application in oral drug delivery to cure IBD. The nanoparticles were simply a polyelectrolyte complex between cationic functionalized chitosan and anionic polysaccharide of Enteromorpha prolifera. Functionalized polysaccharides and the prepared NPs were well characterized. The functionalized nanoparticles could overcome the limitation of poor drug bioavailability and showed a high loading capacity of (45 %) with a controlled release of about (74.5 %). Confocal laser scanning imaging showed higher cellular uptake of the modified nanoparticles than that of the unmodified nanoparticles in LPS-activated RAW 264.7 macrophages and Caco-2 cells. The effect of functionalization on the cellular uptake targetability was assessed using spectrofluorometric measurements after mannose competition. Anti-inflammatory activity of apremilast-loaded NPs is more elevated than the free drug. These results suggest the feasibility of using functionalized EP/Cs nanoparticles in IBD oral drug delivery.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional nanoparticles based on marine polysaccharides for apremilast delivery to inflammatory macrophages: Preparation, targeting ability, and uptake mechanism

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrophobic drug encapsulation inside targeted nanoparticles can enhance accumulation in inflamed sites, limit toxicity to healthy tissue, and improve pharmacokinetics compared to free drug dosing. This study reports a functionalized marine polysaccharide nanoparticle with a controlled release, targeting abilities, and in-situ imaging properties. Carbon dots functionalized Enteromorpha polysaccharide/Mannose/Methionine functionalized Chitosan (CDs.EP/Man/Meth.Cs) NPs could deliver apremilast to inflammatory macrophages and Caco-2 intestinal cells as an in vitro model for application in oral drug delivery to cure IBD. The nanoparticles were simply a polyelectrolyte complex between cationic functionalized chitosan and anionic polysaccharide of Enteromorpha prolifera. Functionalized polysaccharides and the prepared NPs were well characterized. The functionalized nanoparticles could overcome the limitation of poor drug bioavailability and showed a high loading capacity of (45 %) with a controlled release of about (74.5 %). Confocal laser scanning imaging showed higher cellular uptake of the modified nanoparticles than that of the unmodified nanoparticles in LPS-activated RAW 264.7 macrophages and Caco-2 cells. The effect of functionalization on the cellular uptake targetability was assessed using spectrofluorometric measurements after mannose competition. Anti-inflammatory activity of apremilast-loaded NPs is more elevated than the free drug. These results suggest the feasibility of using functionalized EP/Cs nanoparticles in IBD oral drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a syndrome that includes two major diseases, namely Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis, which millions of people are suffering from globally. While the disease incidence is increasing, many pharmacotherapies and treatment scenarios consist of taking one or more medicines for long periods, which may lead to a lack of patient compliance and adverse effects. As a result, creative ways for precise and adequate drug delivery to the inflamed colon over a lengthy period while minimizing the risk of systemic adverse effects are essential for successful IBD therapy [1]. Intestinal macrophages are critical participants in the establishment and maintenance of gut homeostasis. Intestinal macrophage dysregulation leads to a lack of tolerance to the commensal bacteria and also the food antigens, that are considered to generate the chronic inflammation seen in IBD [2].

Accordingly, the attention of scientists in the recent decade has shifted to targeting macrophages as an efficient strategy for curing IBD. Considering the similarities between human intestinal macrophage subsets and mice intestinal macrophages during homeostasis and inflammation [3], murine macrophages RAW 264,7 cell type are usually used for experimental investigation of the efficiency of nanomedicines to promote differentiation toward the anti-inflammatorysssM2 cell type. Overexpression of the inflammatory M1 phenotype of the macrophage cells leads to large quantities production of TNFα and other inflammatory associated cytokines such as IL-6, IL-1, IL-12, and IL-23, as well as a high level of inducible nitric oxide synthase, which promotes tissue damage via nitric oxide synthesis paired with an abundance of reactive oxygen species [4]. Toll-like receptor (TLR) ligands could develop the aforementioned features such as bacterial LPS from E. coli.

Emerging macrophage-directed IBD therapies, such as phosphodiesterase 4 (PDE4) inhibitors (apremilast), boost intracellular cAMP, turning bowel inflammatory macrophages to mature pro-resolving macrophages [5]. However, as an orally administered drug with a hydrophobic nature, Apremilast has a low bioavailability, which mandates an increased dosage/frequency and causes more adverse effects [6]. Polysaccharide-based delivery systems have gained considerable attention as they endow a controlled release to the drug formulations. They also have additional advantages such as large-scale accessibility, relatively inexpensive, chemical stability, low cytotoxicity, and biodegradability [7]. Many polysaccharides nanoformulations have been employed to achieve a controlled release of the effective therapeutic concentration of some beneficial bioactive [8]. In addition, the flexibility of polysaccharides to functionalization has brought progress in their applications, allowing the addition of new properties to serve the purpose for which they are amended and overcome some limitations that might affect their direct use [9].

Enteromorpha prolifera Polysaccharide (EP) is a sulfated polysaccharide exhibiting biological activity such as anticancer, antioxidant, and immunomodulatory properties [10]. Hypolipidemic and hypoglycemic activities [11]. Enteromorpha polysaccharide is rich in carboxyl and sulfate groups, at near-neutral pH, becomes negatively charged, and is accessible to form polyelectrolyte complexes with polycations [12]. Polyelectrolyte complexes simply form between two oppositely charged electrolytes when mixed in solutions. This electrostatic interaction is affected by the ionization degree of poly ions and their charge density, mass ratio, mixing order, polyelectrolytes molecular weights, and interaction time. Particularly as carrier systems for drugs or gene delivery, it has superior advantages such as ease of preparation, low cost, and skipping the need for organic solvents or chemical cross-linking agents during the fabrication [13]. Chitosan (Cs), as a natural polycation with an enzyme-sensitive moiety that may be degraded by β-glycosidase in the colon, has been extensively used either alone or in combinations in colon drug delivery. Chitosan is rich in hydroxyl and amino groups, making it flexible to be functionalized with amino acids or ligands (folic acid or mannose) that endow additional advantages in drug binding, release, steric activities, and targeting ability [7,14].

Fluorescence labeling is among the most common forms of labeling since it allows real-time monitoring of nanoparticle uptake in living cells, for example. It measures nanoparticles inside cells because the fluorescence of cells following nanoparticle uptake may be detected [15]. Carbon dots are a distinct fluorescent category of nanoparticles derived from carbon-based compounds. Their low toxicity, relatively small size, water solubility, and superior photostability have attracted researchers to use them as a potential safe replacement for traditional semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) in drug delivery and bioimaging [16]. Under UV/high energy blue excitation, CDs with green emissions such as citric acid-based CDs are desirable for molecular imaging applications due to the lack of interference with the cellular autofluorescence [17,18].

Herein, a targeted nanoparticulate system based on marine polysaccharides for delivery of apremilast to inflammatory colonic macrophages was prepared. Nanoparticles were easily prepared via polyelectrolyte complexation and showed a high loading efficiency, extended drug release, selectivity to the inflamed cells, and excellent safety to the normal and immune cells. Labeling the nanoparticles with green fluorescent carbon dots showed high amiability to cells, and efficient bioimaging helped qualitatively track the uptake pathway and quantitively measure the approximate number of nanoparticles that entered the cells.

Enteromorpha prolifera green algae were available from the coastal edges of Qingdao city, China. LMW Chitosan (degree of deacetylation ˃ 95 %) was obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Mesochem Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) supplied the drug apremilast. CD3COOD/D2O (E-Merck, 99.9 %). Citric acid was obtained from Shanghai Med Pep Co., Ltd. (China). Methionine, d-(+) mannose, and (ELISA) kits Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay for cytokines TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1 were purchased from Beijing Solarbio (Beijing, China).

Chitosan modification with Mannose (Mann/Cs) was through the reductive amination procedure described previously [19]. Briefly, 30 ml of 1 % chitosan in aqueous acetic acid pH (5.5) was prepared. A solution (10–20 ml) of d-mannose and sodium cyanoborohydride (20 mmol) was dropwise added to the chitosan solution with continuous stirring at 750 rpm for two days, then underwent purification through the dialysis method (cut off 12‑14 KD) for 48 h followed by freeze-drying. Chitosan efficiency of substitution with d-mannose units was calculated from the colorimetric assay of reducing sugars (DNS method). 1H NMR and FTIR were employed to characterize chitosan and the lyophilized mannosylated derivative.

Methionine-modified chitosan (Meth/Cs) was prepared with methionine via Carbodiimide-mediated amide bond formation [20]. Briefly, an aqueous acetic acid solution of 69.66 mM concentration was used to prepare a 1 % chitosan. Similarly, a homogeneous solution of Methionine and EDC (1:1 ratio) in 10 mL H2O. At RT and 750 rpm stirring speed, methionine and EDC aqueous solution were added dropwise into1% chitosan/acetic acid solution and continued to react for three days. 1H NMR and FTIR were employed to characterize chitosan and the lyophilized methionine derivative.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of green fluorescent citric acid carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were synthesized according to [21]. Briefly, a completely clear solution of 1 g of citric acid monohydrate and 3 g of urea in water (15 mL) was prepared. After being heated for around 5 min in a microwave oven (700 W), the obtained black solid was then dissolved in water (60 mL), followed by centrifugation (13,000 rpm, 12 min) then, filtration through a 0.22 μm filter membrane to exclude big particles. The resulting dark brown solution was finally dialyzed for a week against pure water using a membrane dialysis bag (500 Da) to get the citric acid urea carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of EP labeled carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para:
To synthesize CDs/EP to study the cell uptake profile of EP/Cs.NPs, carbodiimide chemistry was followed; EDC.HCl/NHS (1:1) into the EP solution (4 mg/mL). The mixture was 30 min stirred at RT to activate the EP carboxyl groups. Citric acid/urea CDs (0.250 mL−1) were then added and left to react for 24 h. Finally, the solution was purified for 24 h with a dialysis membrane (3500 Da) to remove unlinked CDs, EP, and excess EDC.HCl/NHS, lyophilized and kept until use. Labeling efficiency (%) was determined according to the CDs standard curve, which was depicted in advance (equation y = 0.0116× + 0.2038, (r2 = 0.9819) over a range of 25 μg/mL to 250 μg/mL). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs/EP versus pure CDs using the fluorescence microplate reader (FLx800B, Bio-Tek, USA). Labeling efficiency (%) was determined by dividing the CDs weight over the known number of CDs added to Enteromorpha polysaccharide as follows, Eq. (1):
(1)
Labeling Efficiency

A UV–visible spectrophotometer (TU-1810, Purkinje, China) was used to measure the UV–vis absorbance of CDs and EP/CDs, and a Fluorescence spectrometer (Ex = 420 nm, Em = 520 nm) was used to detect the fluorescence spectrum (Ex = 420 nm, Em = 520 nm) (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan).

Nanoparticles of EP/Cs were generated using a previously reported procedure with slight adjustments [12]. In summary, Cs solution (1 %, pH 3.0) was slowly introduced to (4 mg/mL, pH 6.0) of EP solution at room temperature for 10 min during magnetic stirring at high speed (1000 rpm). Mass ratios from 9:1 to 12:1 and 11:1 to 14:1 (w/w) were prepared for EP/Cs and Mod.EP/Cs.NPs, were then characterized in size, PDI, and zeta potential; according to the results, the 10:1 and 13:1 ratio was chosen as the optimized formula for apremilast loading.

After preparation of Fresh EP/Cs.NPs, apremilast-DMSO solution (1 mg/mL) was added at (2–6 %) of the total NPs weight and incubated for 24 h. The mixture was then sonicated using probe sonication at 40 % power for 10 min under an ice bath to obtain Apr-NPs solution, stored at 4 °C until used. The size distribution, polydispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential of the produced Apr/NPs were measured by DLS (dynamic light scattering) (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK) and are shown in Tables 1 and 2
. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) examined the nanoparticle morphology and surface characteristics.

Apr encapsulated in the NPs was measured by following a method indirectly determined (EE%) and drug loading (DL%) [6]. The freshly prepared NPs were centrifuged for 10 min at 4 °C, 12,000 rpm. UV spectrophotometer set to 229 nm was used to detect the concentration of the unencapsulated drug in the supernatant. Samples were prepared in triplicate, EE% and DL% of Apr in accordance with the calibration curve (equation y = 0.0215× + 0.0176, r2 = 0.9877) over a range of 1 μg/mL to 10 μg/mL were estimated using the following Eqs. (2) and (3):

Dialysis diffusion method initiated at pH 6.8 (pH in both the ileum and colon) was used for In vitro drug release. In a water bath (60 rpm stirring, at 37 °C), pure apremilast and Apr/NPs (4 mL) were suspended in 30 mL of the buffer system for 24 h. To aid the solubilization of apremilast released from NPs in the release medium, Tween-80 (2 % w/v) was added. At certain intervals (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 12, and 24 h), 400 μL of the dissolution medium were taken (immediately replaced with a comparable volume of fresh medium), centrifugation for 10 min at 12,000 rpm was done. A spectrophotometer measured the quantity of apremilast produced from the NPs in supernatants. Apremilast releasing rate was assessed by the Eq. (4):

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPPH free radical scavenging activity of the prepared NPs
ce:section.ce:para:
DPPH scavenging colorimetric assay was performed to evaluate the antioxidant activity of NPs [22]. Nanoparticles without carbon dots labeling were used; ascorbic acid is a positive control. The nanoparticles were diluted in ethanol to produce a concentration of (500 μg/mL), which was then combined with 100 L of ethanolic DPPH to generate a final NPs concentration of (250 μg/mL) and ascorbic acid concentrations of (25 μg/mL) on a 96 well plate. The plates were placed at room temperature for 30 min in the dark before being read in a microplate reader (FLx800B, Bio-Tek, USA) at 517 nm. Calculation of the DPPH inhibitory activity was done according to the following formula, Eq. (5).
(5)
DPPH scavenging percentage
%
=
control
A 0 . − sample
A 1 .
control
A 0 .
× 100
where the control A0. and sample A1. are the DPPH absorbance's without NPs and with the NPs, respectively.

Raw 264.7 and Caco-2 cells were provided from the Cell line bank in Shanghai, cultured in complete DMEM medium, and 1 % antibiotics (Solarbio, China). The cells were cultured in a humidified atmosphere with 5 % CO2 at 37 °C.

Cell Viability Assays for carbon dots, nanoparticles, and Apr/NPs were assessed by performing the MTT assay; in brief, Caco-2 or RAW 264.7 cells (1 × 105) per well in 96 well plates were cultured at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 in a humidified chamber until complete adhesion, after that, serially increasing concentrations of CDs, NPs and Apr/NPs in serum-free medium were added gently to the cells and incubated for 24 h then, cell viability determination was completed as previously described [23].

Laser Confocal microscopy was used to follow the cellular uptake of the Mod.EP/Cs.NPs and EP/Cs.NPs (Revolve FL, Echo, USA). As follows, Raw 264.7 cells at a density of 1 × 104 were seeded on confocal plates for 12 h at 37 °C, in the humidified chamber. To obtain the inflamed cell model, LPS (1 μg/mL) was added for 6 h, LPS-containing medium was discarded, and 1 mL of (150 μg/mL) Mod.NPs and EP/Cs.NPs in serum-free medium were incubated for 3–4 h at 37 °C. Then, cells were washed carefully with cold PBS (pH 7.4) three times, fixed with a solution of 4 % paraformaldehyde, and kept at −4 °C until observed. For cell visualization after competitive mannose inhibition, free mannose at concentrations of 200 and 400 (μg/mL) in a serum-free medium was added 1 h before the nanoparticle's addition [24].

As previously mentioned in the visualization study, the experiment was conducted on 96 well plate; after washing with PBS, cells were lysed with 100 μL of 0.5 % Triton X-100 in PBS. A multimode microplate reader was set (Ex 420 nm, Em 520 nm) to measure the intracellular fluorescence intensity. Free mannose was supplied as described above to demonstrate mannose receptor-mediated internalization. To quantify uptake, the proportion of cells associated with fluorescence was divided by the quantity of fluorescence in the feed solution.

In order to determine the uptake mechanism of EP/Cs.NPs into RAW 264.7 cells, different chemical inhibitors for 1 h were added at 37 °C to previously one-night incubated cells in 96-well plates. Then, CDs/NPs were added. After 4 h, the cells were washed with cold PBS. Then, the multimode microplate reader was used to determine the effect of inhibitors on NPs uptake quantitatively. The chemical inhibitors applied in the experiment included d-mannose (200, 400 μg/mL); sodium azide: energy-dependent endocytosis inhibitor at a concentration (0.1 %); CPZ: a Clathrin-mediated endocytosis inhibitor at (7 μg/mL); Nocodazole: inhibitor of Micropinocytosis at a concentration (5 μg/mL) in addition to the pre-incubation at 4 °C for a separate plate. The control (100 %) was the fluorescence intensity in the cells without endocytic inhibitors [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the anti-inflammatory activity of nanoparticles and Apr/NPs in RAW 264.7 macrophages

ce:section.ce:para: RAW 264.7 in the same culturing scenario for MTT assay was performed except for adding LPS (1 μg/ml) for 16 h. The Griess reaction was performed to determine the nitrite content in the culture medium, which is an indication of NO production. The cells were treated for 24 h with NPs (150 μg/mL) or Apr/NPs (10 μg/mL). Following this, the medium (100 L) was combined with an equivalent volume of Griess reagent combination (reagent A: 1 % sulfanilamide in 5 % phosphoric acid, reagent B: 0.1 % naphthyl ethylenediamine dihydrochloride aqueous). The generation of NO was then measured using a microplate plate reader at 540 nm.

TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 production assay confirmed the anti-inflammatory activity of nanoparticles and Apr/NPs in RAW 264.7 macrophages, cells sample in 96-well plate incubated for 12 h at 37 °C in a humidified 5 % CO2 incubator. Then inflamed with LPS (1 μg/mL) for 12 h; after removal of LPS, 200 μL samples were added for 12 h. finally, 100 μL of the supernatant was collected and centrifuged (TGL-20M, Xiangyi, China) at 2000 rpm for 20 min at 4 °C. IL-6, IL-1, and TNFα-levels were measured using ELISA kits (Solarbio Beijing, China).

Caco-2 cells between the 15 and 25th passages were used in the present study. Quantification of Caco-2 Cellular uptake of NPs was according to the intensity of fluorescence measured by the microplate reader (FLx800B, Bio-Tek, USA) [26]. Caco-2 cells were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for one week. Then, the culture medium was removed and transport buffer (HBSS, pH 7.4) was added for 30 min at 37 °C. The nanoparticle's cellular uptake was triggered by replacing HBSS with 250 μg/mL of the nanoparticle in HBSS, pH 7.4 for 3 h. The experiment ended by washing the cells with ice-cold (PBS, pH 7.4) to remove any nanoparticles not taken by the cells. The cells were lysed with 0.5 % Triton X-100 to quantify the internalized nanoparticles in the cell lysate using a microplate reader (Ex 420 nm, Em 520 nm). The proportion of the cell-associated fluorescence vs. the quantity of fluorescence of the original feed solution was used to quantify uptake. For Caco-2 cellular visualization using Confocal laser scanning microscopy, as mentioned previously except for, after cell wash with PBS to eliminate the untaken nanoparticles by the cells. Cells were then fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min. The plates were kept at −4 °C until observed by the confocal laser scanning microscope (Revolve FL, Echo, USA).

FTIR spectra (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA) were employed to analyze the chemical structure of materials. A thermogravimetric analyzer was used to generate DSC patterns (STA-200 instruments, Beijing, China). The XRD patterns were taken using a Bruker D8 Advance (Bruker AXS, Germany) equipped with a graphite monochromator and Cu K radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of methionine and mannose functionalized chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan functionalization aimed to create a chemically active, stable molecule with enough binding forces by inserting chemically active sites in the generated form. Chitosan was modified with methionine (Meth) via amide bond formation among its free primary carboxyl and amino groups on methionine using green carbodiimide chemistry. To generate the desired product, the carboxyl group was activated using EDC/NHS and interacted with chitosan. Methionine was presumed to generate weak electrostatic poles in Methi-functionalized chitosan to promote optimal drug binding for safer delivery to the desired locations as drug transporters in the body. Because of the compatibility between the S atom of the drug carriers and the S atom of the drug, our drug with the S atom would be most appropriate for binding and release [20]. This was visible in the entrapment efficiency of nanoparticles and the drug loading content, which rose by 1–4 % and 0.1–1 %, respectively, compared to unfunctionalized nanoparticles, as explained in Table 2.

Chitosan was modified with mannose to improve their uptake by inflammatory macrophages via receptor-mediated endocytosis. Mannosylation of chitosan was via reductive amination with d-mannose in sodium triacetoxyborohydride [NaBH (OAc)3]. This solution is a mild reductive aminating agent with remarkable selectivity to reduce aldehydes rather than ketones [27]. Mannose's degree of substitution (DS) was estimated by colorimetric assay of reducing sugars (DNS method) to be 48.13 %. In the FTIR Fig. 1 (a) Mann-functionalized chitosan displayed the distinctive mannose bands at 808 cm−ˡ and 890 cm−ˡ, COO symmetric stretching at 1417 cm−ˡ, and COO asymmetric stretching vibration at 1626 cm−ˡ confirming chitosan functionalization with d-mannose. Fig. 1 (b) In methionine functionalized chitosan, an amide group was formed by combining two amino groups. A new stretching frequency for the terminal alkyl chain was produced at 2925 cm−l, confirming the structure confirmation of the Cs functionalized methionine through EDC chemistry. The characteristic peak of amide groups at 1597 cm−ˡ in Meth-functionalized chitosan demonstrated that methionine was connected to the amino group of Cs via an amide bond.

The 1H NMR spectra for chitosan, methionine, and mannose-modified chitosan are shown in (Fig. 2 ); Chitosan's proton assignment (Cs) (a) was as follows (ppm): δ1.96 = CH3 (chitosan acetyl group); δ2.87 = CH (H-2 of chitosan); δ3.5–3.9 = CH + CH2 (H 3,4,5 and 6 of chitosan); δ4.6 = CH (H 1 of chitosan) [28]. Whereas the Meth-Cs (b) exhibited a distinct peak of δ 4.68 for the amino group attached to an a-C atom of methionine found in methionine functionalized chitosan. The product structure was confirmed by the peak at a d value of δ 3.26 [20]. Consequently, the production of an amide bond was proven by 1HNMR spectra. As well in the mannose modified chitosan (c), the peaks and changes in the 1H NMR spectrum of Mann-Cs compared to those of Cs provide convincing evidence of mannosylation. A particularly high peak at 1.89 ppm might be attributed to the proton of CH2, which served as a linkage between Cs and mannose.

In the case of Cs, a strong peak at 2.5 ppm was identified owing to the proton located at the chitosan ring to which the NH2 group is attached, which was shifted to a weak signal at 2.49 ppm in the case of Mann-Cs due to sterically blocked proton by the mannose moiety. Furthermore, a prominent signal at 1.89 ppm in contrast to Cs gives clear evidence of mannosylation owing to protons supplied by the mannose group in Mann-Cs that are lacking in chitosan [29]. DSC specifically revealed the transitions induced by heat in the Cs, Meth-Cs, and mannose modified chitosan structures Fig. 1 (c). The endothermic peak at 77.1 °C in Cs was connected with water loss, while the exothermic peak at 289.9 °C was associated with deacetylation as well as degradation of Cs. As for the Meth-Cs and Mann-Cs, the endothermic peaks moved to 114.2 and 66.4 °C, respectively. This could be related to the fact that the functionalized groups of Cs and water have significant interactions. Because their peaks were closer together, both Mann-Cs and Meth-Cs showed lower thermal stabilities than Cs. 284.2 and 249.6 °C, lower than Cs (289.9 °C). Thus, The DSC results backed up the molecular level of the inclusion and interaction complex development in the modified Cs. The high energy amorphous dispersion was linked to the lack of endothermic peaks and crystalline in the DSC of chitosan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent carbon dots and CDs/EP characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed the morphology of CDs to be separate quasi-spheres. CDs are relatively homogeneous in size, with an average diameter of 2.4 nm and a distribution range of 1.9–4.1 nm, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The absorbance at 420 nm produces the highest fluorescence intensity at 520 nm, and the fluorescence at 520 nm is the maximum in the initial neutral state [21], Fig. 3 (b), (c). FTIR results of CDs and CDs/EP reveal that; the peaks at 1195, 3260, and 3401 cm−l are related to stretching vibrations of CO, OH, and CH, respectively. The stretching vibrations of CO and NH bonds may be assigned to the peaks at 1715, 1604, and 3401 cm−l, respectively, indicating the presence of amide surface functional groups of CDs, as shown in Fig. 3 (d). The characteristic peaks of the modified CDs/EP nanoparticles at 1648 and 1233 cm−ˡ were assigned to EP protonated carboxyl and sulfate groups, indicating the electrostatic interaction between both compounds. Labeling efficiency was calculated from Eq. (1) mentioned in the Methods section to be 29.3 %. Fig. 3 (e) as shown: CDs show a characteristic XRD peak at 24.4, corresponding to the graphite crystalline structure. Results indicate that after EP modification with CDs, there is no change in the crystalline phase of EP. The appearance of effective broad peaks in both EP and CDs/EP nanoparticles indicates that the samples are nanocrystalline in nature.
The electrostatic interaction among modified chitosan amino groups and sulfate or carboxyl groups of EP was prominent in the production of nanoparticles.

As illustrated in the FTIR Fig. 4 (a), the amide I and II characteristic peaks of Cs were at 1634 and 1560 cm−ˡ. EP peaks at 1632, 1255, and 849 cm−l were assigned to CO, SO, and 3-sulfate rhamnose stretching vibrations. The amide bands in the EP/Cs NPs were hidden by the 1623 cm−l bending band, and a new band arose at 1520 cm−l, which was ascribed to NH3+ groups. The peak of 1255 cm−ˡ was attributed to sulfate groups of EP. In addition, the OH stretching shifted from 3390 cm−ˡ to 3375 cm−ˡ, showing the presence of H-bonding contact and polyelectrolyte complexation between EP and Cs [12]. The FTIR spectra of apremilast, blank NPs, and Apr/NPs were observed to further confirm if apremilast was properly encapsulated, Fig. 4 (b), (c); The existence of various functional amide and ketone carbonyl groups (NHCOCH3, CO) in the fingerprint area was confirmed by the prominent peaks ascribed to pure Apr. The stretching vibration of the ketone (CO), amide (NH), and amide CO groups of Apr resulted in peaks at 1766, 3361, and 1685 cm−ˡ in the spectra, respectively. There is no significant shifting of peaks in the spectrum of the produced NPs; however, there is a decrease in the intensity of the peaks in the drug's fingerprint area. Apr was encapsulated in an amorphous form inside EP/Cs polymeric nanoparticles. These findings indicated the possibility of hydrogen-bonding interaction between apremilast (amide and ketone carbonyl groups) and EP/Cs NPs (the amino and hydroxyl groups). These interactions aided apremilast's regulated release [6]. The heterogeneity of dried materials was assessed by the X-ray diffraction pattern. The peaks corresponding to EP and Cs in EP/Cs NPs were attenuated, as seen in Fig. 4 (d). Because the electrostatic contact between amino and sulfate groups (or carboxyl groups) disrupted the intra- and intermolecular hydrogen bonding relationship of EP and Cs, which began their crystallization. Apremilast, a very hydrophobic medication, is mixed into the aqueous solution, which prefers to crystallize. When apremilast crystals grow within the EP/Cs NPs, the drug's release pattern will be patchy. Nevertheless, because to the hydrogen-bonding interaction between EP/Cs NPs and apremilast, no crystalline peaks were observed in the Apr/NPs. So, the amorphous or disordered-crystalline form of apremilast on the nanoparticles was verified.

As shown in Tables 1 and 2, DLS was used to assess the particle size and zeta potential of blank NPs and Apr/NPs. The size and PDI of the blank nanoparticles were 268.6 ± 2.9 nm and 0.12 ± 0.02, and 280.6 ± 6.3 and 0.18 ± 0.02, respectively, while those of Apr/NPs and Apr/Mod.NPs were 314.6 ± 4.1 nm and 0.1 ± 0.023, and 329.2 ± 4.6 and 0.19 ± 0.05, respectively. When compared with blank NPs, the mean particle size and PDI of Apr/NPs enhanced considerably, showing that apremilast was effectively encapsulated. The illustration depicts images of NPs with the same apremilast dosage. The inset illustrates that Apr/NPs and Apr/Mod.NPs solutions were transparent white and yellowish-white, respectively, revealing that apremilast's solubility was greatly enhanced since being incorporated into the nanoparticles. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of blank NPs and Mod/NPs compared to Apr/NPs and Apr/Mod.NPs are −15.2 ± 0.25 and −20.5 ± 1.39 to −16.8 ± 2.56 and −21.8 ± 1.2, respectively. Fig. 6 (a), and (b) display TEM images indicating that the nanoparticles were circular and well diffused without agglomeration. Additionally, the particle size obtained by TEM was less than that determined by DLS, owing to the shrinkage of nanoparticles during water loss (Fig. 5 ) [30,31].

In vitro studies of apremilast release from the nanoparticles were carried out to identify how the nanoparticle delivery behavior differs from that of the pure drug. The drug release profile of pure Apr and Apr loaded NPs is provided in Fig. 6 (c). The drug release in the media showed a biphasic pattern. The cumulative release of apremilast from the nanoparticles was 74.5 %, while the release of pure apremilast reached its plateau at 8 h with 43.2 %, consistent with the previous reports [6]. The drug burst release from nanoparticles might be due to the easy surface dispersion of surface-adsorbed drugs as well as nanosized particles in the media due to their higher surface area. The encapsulated drug inside the nanoparticles was likely released via diffusion, resulting in the gradual phase release. The drug's long-acting release may aid in reducing the number of times it needs to be taken orally [6].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular viability assay for CDs and NPs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs are generally safe for cells, as mentioned in similar studies [32,33]. Nevertheless, before starting the cell experiments, the effect of the prepared CDs on RAW 264.7 was studied at different concentrations up to 125 μg/mL, Fig. 7 (a). The results showed high compatibility of CDs toward the tested cells across the entire concentration range, even for 48 h of continuous exposure. These results clearly show that CDs can be used as a safe probe for cellular and in vivo imaging applications.

We next examined the viability of the cells after being pretreated with EP/Cs nanoparticles. Here we tested two models for IBD encountered cells, RAW 264.7 macrophages (immune cells) and Caco-2 intestinal endothelial cells, Fig. 7 (b), (c), respectively. Both cell types showed high tolerability toward nanoparticles. However, compared to the epithelial cell type, immune cells showed a higher sensitivity when the concentration was higher than 150 μg/mL, so we continued for 200 μg/mL in Caco-2 [34]. After drug loading in the nanoparticles, the viability was tested again in RAW264.7, whereas Apr/NPs exhibited enhanced safety of the drug after loading in NPs as compared to a previous report [35], Fig. 7 (d). The viability of RAW 264.7 cells was unaffected at a dosage of 10 μg/mL of Apr loaded NPs, but started to decline when exposed to higher concentrations. While for the free drug the viability started to decline markedly after the 7.5 μg/mL. Thus, we used Apr/NPs at the 10 μg/mL concentration in all cell experiments. On the basis of the results, our nanoparticles are of high safety and that was anticipated as some Asian countries with coastal edges consume Enteromorpha green algae in their food, here we are using the polysaccharide extracted from it as the major component of nanoparticles [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inflammatory cellular uptake in vitro investigation
ce:section.ce:para: Murine peritoneal macrophage (Raw 264.7) cells were utilized as a model to detect the targeting ability of CDs/NPs along with the study. TNF-α and inflammatory mediators are secreted by abnormally activated macrophages, and they significantly influence the pathophysiology of IBD, particularly in Crohn's disease [34,37].

Lipopolysaccharides (LPS) (1 μg/mL) were used to stimulate macrophages to secrete proinflammatory cytokines [38]. ELISA assay investigated the feasibility of the used concentration of LPS to prepare model cells in a condition similar to that used for every experiment in the study. The stimulated macrophages secreted (2.24 ± 0.06 ng/L) of TNF-α, which was double that in the untreated group (1.54 ± 0.158 ng/L), so the in vitro inflammatory model was ready. Here we try to know how inflammation affected CDs/NPs absorption in untreated and activated macrophages. In Fig. 8 , images indicate that CDs/NPs (green fluorescence) were primarily up taken into the periphery of the cytoplasm and nucleus of macrophage. Fluorescent signals could be detected in the case of the modified NPs treated with activated macrophages than the unmodified ones. In the quantification experiment (Fig. 9 c), the internalized amount of the modified NPs was more than that of the unmodified ones. The activated cells were significantly higher than the control cells (untreated). It can be explained by the upregulation of mannose receptors on the surface of the activated macrophage increased the modified NPs internalization efficacy compared to unmodified and inactivated cells.

Nevertheless, nanoparticles were generally internalized via mannose receptor, consistent with what was mentioned in a related study, EP polysaccharides internalized through mannose receptor [39]. However, the enhanced internalization in the modified NPs was due to ligand addition to the nanoparticles, which endows higher targetability. In brief, during the inflammatory condition, the designed negatively charged, small-sized, and ligand functionalized NPs preferentially bind to injured tissue which is rich in positively charged proteins via electrostatic interaction, showing improved adherence and more manageable penetration [40]. It is worth mentioning that, the selection of nanoparticle material is critically significant in regulating and directing the subsequent reaction associated with nanoparticle internalization. Surface charge and nanoparticles particle shape play a role in the following downstream signaling, nanoparticles synthesized of biocompatible components has a modulating effect, and it is stated that nano particulates without stimulating or tolerizing added moieties exhibit anti-inflammatory effects in the preclinical therapy of inflammatory bowel disease. Here, nanoparticles successful delivery to inflammatory macrophages has been translated into enhanced anti-inflammatory activity for the drug result from macrophage modulation from inflammatory to anti-inflammatory macrophages that will be mentioned later in the part of cytokines inhibitory assay by ELISA [41,42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake after competition with mannose
ce:section.ce:para: One of the main problems in achieving high therapeutic efficacy without hurting healthy tissue is targeted drug delivery to macrophages. Mannosylated NPs are an example of how a macrophage can be targeted with nanoparticles to provide particular pathway agonists or inhibitors to differentiate myeloid cells CD206+ inflammatory into pro-resolving macrophages [5]. To investigate the targeting efficacy of the prepared NPs free mannose at Conc. (200, 400 μg/mL) medium was added to the cells 30 min before the CDs/NPs, either quantitively Fig. 9 (d) using the microplate reader or qualitative in the confocal images Fig. 9 (a), (b); it was clear that the fluorescence became weaker in both cases of NPs incubation in a concentration-dependent manner. Because mannose may disrupt the binding attributable to competitive inhibition, our findings explain why EP/Cs NPs mediated transport was mannose receptor-dependent [14]. Nevertheless, preferential uptake through the mannose receptor doesn't interfere with entrance through other lectin receptors that could recognize carbohydrate bearing domains, here the meaning is that our nanoparticles could be internalized through other polysaccharide receptors such as Dectin-1 and SR. However, noteworthy to mention about Dectin-1 receptors are mostly recognized structures of β-glucan which is especially abundant in the cell wall of fungi [43]. In addition, SR receptors are involved in uptake mediation of the modified low-density lipoprotein (LDL) by arterial wall macrophages, products of bacterial cell wall like lipoteichoic acid (LTA) in addition to other several apoptotic thymocytes which are not the main interest of the study [44,45].

To better understand the cellular uptake of EP/Cs NPs under various conditions, mechanical aspects associated with nanoparticle uptake by macrophage cells were explored. To explore the involvement of endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, and micropinocytosis in NPs cellular uptake, three types of endocytosis inhibitors were chosen: sodium azide and chlorpromazine hydrochloride (CPZ), and nocodazole. The initial study aimed to discover if NPs uptake process by cells was energy-dependent. The findings were favorable, At 4 °C, cellular transport of NPs declined by nearly 80 %, as seen in Fig. 9 (e), indicating that macrophage cell uptake of the nanoparticles is energy-dependent. The cells were subsequently treated with 150 μg/mL of bare NPs for 4 h after 30 min of pre-incubation with recognized endocytosis inhibitors such as clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CPZ) or macropinocytosis (nocodazole). The inhibitors partially blocked the pathways with a lesser overall decrease in the case of unfunctionalized nanoparticles demonstrating that EP/Cs NPs could enter the cell via one or more of the endocytic uptake pathways [46]. Once entered the cells; the endolysosomal network within plasma-membrane bounded vesicles transfers and trafficks the nanoparticles until they arrive at their target. It is hard to completely describe the intracellular consequences that take place during nanoparticle movement inside these vesicles as it is a complicated trafficking path. Diverse intracellular compartments can be the terminus of NPs journey inside the cells: endosome or lysosome, nucleus, endoplasmic reticulum, mitochondria, and Golgi apparatus are all among the destinations where the drug release can be triggered. However, at this level of analysis, we suspect the involvement of lysosome and mitochondria pathways in NPs uptake because lysosomes are concerned with the ingestion of the taken-up foreign material by the cells from the outside. In addition, there is another probability of cytoplasmic triggered drug release through the macropinocytosis pathway as it transports the NPs cargo direct to the endoplasmic reticulum and/or Golgi [45,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of ROS, NOS, cytokines inhibitory activity of bare and Apr loaded NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In this group of experiments, Fig. 10 bare and Apr loaded NPs have tested if the natural polysaccharides NPs could have a role in the inflammation treatment. Results Fig. 10 (a) demonstrated that EP/Cs NPs showed strong DPPH radical scavenging activity at the established concentrations EP/Cs NPs (250 μg/mL), suggesting that EP/Cs NPs as natural antioxidants can work against reactive oxygen species, which are one of the main contributors to inflammatory bowel disease. The NPs were relatively non-toxic to macrophage cells at a high level of concentration. NPs with or without drug neutralized the LPS-induced NOS production to some extent Fig. 10 (b); when loaded with the drug, both ROS and NOS neutralization increased. NO has decreased from 11.64 ± 0.016 to 4.913 ± 0.002 and 2.89423 ± 0.005 for bare and Apr loaded Mod.NPs and 5.59 ± 0.002 and 3.47 ± 0.056 for bare and Apr loaded NPs. This place natural marine polysaccharides-based nanoparticles in a category of possible nanocarriers resulting in synergistic treatment and control of inflammatory bowel disease. The possible therapeutic value of the NPs is unknown and needs more investigation, which is not the aim of this study. But the presence of phytochemical compounds such as amine groups and carboxylic acids may be the source of radical scavenging activity and NOS neutralization. Inhibition of inflammatory cytokines Fig. 10 (c, d, e), including TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6, which influence the development of IBD were not surprising as expected from the ROS and NOS inhibitory activities results. For bare and Apr loaded NPs; TNF-α has decreased from 2.24 ± 0.06 to 1.71 ± 0.051 and 1.62 ± 0.03 ng/L, respectively, IL-6 from 46.69 ± 0.35 to 21.07 ± 0.99 and 18.93 ± 0.41 pg/mL, respectively, and IL-1β from 40.22 ± 2.36 to 16.79 ± 0.89 and 14.27 ± 1.4 ng/L, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake of NPs in Caco-2 intestinal cell model
ce:section.ce:para: Upon designing NPs for different applications, we should put into consideration the target cell's microenvironment, since it markedly determines the action of NPs, defines where they pass and which type of cells they will interact with. Gastrointestinal tract (GIT) microenvironment and intestinal epithelium's poor permeability are crucial in deciding nanoparticle's fate or whether they will cross via transcytosis the intestinal epithelium or degrade. Therefore, the close similarity of Caco-2 cells to the intestinal epithelial cells makes them extremely useful for studying intestinal diseases. Caco-2 cells when differentiated show identical characteristics to the enterocytes of the small bowel. They are useful as an in vitro model for the intestinal barrier because upon forming a differentiated monolayer, exhibit the absorptive enterocyte's typical properties [48,49].

Our NPs have a size range that is conducive to intestinal absorption. The relationship between intestinal absorption and particle size has been investigated previously; it is stated that the particles with dimensions of 100 to 500 nm are probably endocytosed, or diffused through the submucosal layer allowing them to enter the cell [50]. As previously identified, the amount of green fluorescence released by CDs has been deemed a marker of CDs/NPs uptake in Caco-2 cells [51]. Cellular uptake quantitative analysis depended on the fluorescence intensity of cell lysates compared to the feeding solution fluorescence intensity measured with a multimode microplate reader. The concentration of nanoparticles for the experiment was 250 μg/mL in HEPES buffer. The quantity of fluorescence in the feed solution was used to calculate uptake quantification percentage compared to the quantity of fluorescence linked to cells to be 74.34 %, 42.96 % for CDs/Mod.NPs and CDs/NPs respectively Fig. 11 (c). As observed in Fig. 11 (a, b), despite some noticeably seen areas without fluorescence due to the multilayer of cells. A higher green fluorescent signal could be observed in cells with CDs/Mod.NPs, indicating successful entry into the cell.

Physicochemical properties of small molecule drugs mostly influence their delivery, and consequently bioavailability. Delivery efforts focus on solubility problems amelioration, controlling drug release, and thus adjusting their pharmacokinetics. Stability and intracellular targeted delivery are additional advantages that could enable the enhanced delivery of therapeutics to the target site. Accordingly, multifunctional nanoparticle development where additional features like targeting and imaging properties granted to the nanoparticles may help in the well translation of these features to enhanced patient compliance and improved clinical outcomes.

Here, we have introduced a multifunctional nanoparticle of marine polysaccharides provided a controlled release formula of apremilast. Nanoparticles have met all the advantages of a successful drug carrier of well-controlled nano-scale particle size with narrow dispersity and high drug loading capacity. Surface negativity and ligand modification made the nanoparticles specific to the inflamed region with targeting ability to inflamed macrophages. Excellent in situ imaging properties of the green CDs helped track the cellular uptake and clearly evidenced NPs localization in the cell nucleus and peripheral cytoplasm. For a better understanding, a comprehensive investigation of their cellular behaviors and mechanisms of internalization was done; CDs/nanoparticles with or without modifications have been chosen for evaluations on their uptakes by inflammatory macrophages and colonic epithelial cells as a model for inflammatory bowel diseases. Results showed the nanocarrier compatibility with the cells. RAW macrophage cells could internalize both nanoparticles in a similar pathway, with slightly higher internalization efficiency for the modified nanoparticles. Accordingly, a novel functionalized marine polysaccharide nanoparticle as an inflammatory macrophage-targeted drug delivery system represents a potential therapeutic strategy for IBD treatment. Evidently, our work has represented only the cellular model experiments which need further studies in animal models for complete authentication before introducing such trials to the clinic.
